title: Marine heatwave likely cause of mass fish deaths on WA's Pilbara coast, authorities say - ABC News
description: Authorities believe marine heatwave conditions may have led to the death of around 30,000 fish at Gnoorea Beach, south of Karratha.
bodyText: Tue 28 Jan 2025 at 9:06am Fisheries officials say a marine heatwave may have led to a fish kill on Western Australia's Pilbara coast. About 30,000 fish were found dead at Gnoorea Beach near Karratha. The marine heatwave is expected to continue throughout January and February amid warnings of "potentially catastrophic" effects. Authorities believe a climate-change-driven marine heatwave is the most likely cause of mass fish deaths off Western Australia's Pilbara coast. Experts say it is reminiscent of one of the most severe marine warming events ever recorded in the state and fear further damage may be done. More than 30,000 fish washed up dead at Gnoorea Beach, about 1,500 kilometres north of Perth, earlier this month in what a local described as a "devastating, eerie" scene. Thousand of fish, including dory, rock cod, and puffer fish, were found dead. (Supplied: DPIRD) Department of Primary Industries and Regional Development (DPIRD) spokesman Nathan Harrison said severe marine heatwave conditions had been observed off WA's north-west coast in recent weeks. The phenomenon was detected in September last year and declared a moderate heatwave, but conditions have warmed since then. A map showing the temperature difference from the climatological average for sea surface temperatures for January 6 to 12, 2025. (Supplied: Bureau of Meteorology) "It is possible that prolonged thermal stress due to the ocean conditions is associated with the fish kill," Mr Harrison said. "Following the recent tropical cyclone, the marine heatwave strength has declined. However, we are moving into the higher-risk summer months." Locals have described being speechless on discovering the mass fish kill. (Supplied) Sea surface temperatures are about 4 to 5 degrees Celsius above the long-term average in the north coast bioregion and 2–3C more in the Gascoyne bioregion. The Bureau of Meteorology expects marine heatwave conditions to continue throughout January and February. Similar temperatures in the summer of 2010–11 wiped out about 1,300 square kilometres of seagrass in World-Heritage-listed Shark Bay. "That's where we may have potentially catastrophic impacts on places like Shark Bay or further down the coast," Mr Harrison said. He said the effects of the 2010–11 heatwave were still being felt in commercial fisheries, where there were major losses to Roe's abalone stocks and scallops in the Gascoyne. Coastal oceanographer Charitha Pattiaratchi leads a University of WA team that is designing ocean gliders. The researchers are collaborating with state authorities to gather data about the marine heatwave. In December last year, a marine drone was deployed from the Pilbara town of Onslow and what was discovered suggested the hotter-than-average water temperature extended about 200 metres below the surface. UWA's Charitha Pattiaratchi says more evidence is needed to determine the heatwave's impact. (ABC News) Two more missions will be conducted over the next month to get a clearer picture of sub-surface ocean temperatures. "Usually the heatwaves are on the surface … when the winds start up, it can actually mix the water, so if it's cold water underneath, it will actually reduce the heatwave," Dr Pattiaratchi said. The underwater drones will be piloted via satellites. (ABC News: Glyn Jones) He said the heatwave's longevity was closely tied to the development of a rare summer La Niña. Cooling in the tropical Pacific Ocean strengthens the Leeuwin Current, which snakes along the west coast and drags hot water from north to south. The ocean current tends to peak during the winter months but can pick up speed into February and March and dump unseasonally warm water on unaccustomed ecosystems. "It's very much related to climate change," Dr Pattiaratchi said. Sean McNeair says the 2010/11 marine heatwave has had devastating effects. (ABC Pilbara: Alistair Bates) Malgana man Sean McNeair lives and works on the waters of Shark Bay (Gathaagudu) as the offshore manager at the sea cucumber harvester Tidal Moon. The Indigenous-owned business was recently awarded a permit to replant local seagrass as part of a community-led movement trialling restorative methods. Mr McNeair described the heatwave as a "terrifying" threat to his livelihood and cultural obligations. "We don't see things as individual, so everything is linked together," he said. Organisations such as Tidal Moon have been working to restore seagrass meadows. (Supplied: Tidal Moon) Traditional knowledge and contemporary scientific research have shown seagrass plays a critical role in the region's biodiversity, acting as a habitat, food source, and carbon sink. "If country's not being looked after and country's sick, we're also sick," Mr McNeair said. Paul Gamblin says the heatwave should inspire efforts to combat climate change. (ABC News: Glyn Jones) Paul Gamblin, director at the Australian Marine Conservation Society, called for greater transparency and regular updates from state and federal governments about the developing heatwave. He said the circumstances reiterated a need to combat climate change and halt oil and gas projects. "What's happening in our blue backyard this summer is something we really need to heed," Mr Gamblin said.
